text: Today, we will present an interesting case of a large retrorectal schwannoma resected via a minimally invasive approach.
The patient is a forty-nine-year-old female evaluated for a large pelvic mass found incidentally during attempted IUD exchange.
She was largely asymptomatic, but a thorough review of systems identified a year-long history of urinary incontinence as well as abdominal distension and bloating.
She denied any changes in bowel habits.
Her history was significant for prior abdominoplasty.
A preoperative CT of the abdomen and pelvis demonstrated a large heterogeneous retrorectal mass.
A pelvic MRI further demonstrated a bilobed solid mass with cystic changes or necrosis.
There was no evidence of invasion into surrounding structures or pathologic lymph nodes.
The image shows anterior displacement of the bladder, uterus, and rectum, highlighted with a red circle.
A CT-guided parasacral biopsy demonstrated a low-grade spindle cell neoplasm consistent with schwannoma.
Given its location and size, we opted to approach this resection transabdominally using the XI robot.
The patient is positioned for surgery with the robot positioned on the patient's left side, prepared for a pelvic procedure.
The surgeon places robotic ports in a transverse orientation approximately three to four centimeters above the umbilicus, preparing for the transabdominal approach.
The surgical team plans for a lower midline or Pfannenstiel incision to facilitate the extraction of the tumor.
Preoperative cystoscopy is performed, with bilateral uterine stents placed and instilled with endocyanin green, aiding in the visual identification of structures during the procedure.
Upon abdominal entry and insufflation, a large mass is visible, occupying the pelvic region.
The surgeon retracts the uterus anteriorly, enhancing visualization of the pelvic region during the robotic-assisted procedure.
The surgeon opens the retroperitoneum to the right of the large pelvic mass, extending the dissection laterally.
Firefly technology is utilized to visually identify the ureter during the surgical procedure, enhancing the visibility of the ureteral structures.
The surgeon continues lateral dissection, confirming the location of the ureter, to expose the right side of the large pelvic mass.
The surgeon dissects circumferentially around the large pelvic mass, staying close to its capsule, with the uterus retracted anteriorly for better visualization.
The surgeon displaces the sigmoid colon anteriorly and laterally, consistent with preoperative imaging, to improve access and visualization of the surgical field.
The left ureter is visualized along the left pelvic sidewall, clearly identifiable against the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon frees the mass circumferentially at its superior aspect, increasing its mobility, and continues the dissection distally.
The surgeon performs lateral and interior dissection down to the pelvic floor, reaching the distal extent of the mass, ensuring careful separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon confirms the location of the mass using a digital rectal exam, ensuring accurate positioning within the surgical field.
The surgeon encounters difficulty with the posterior dissection due to the large size and bulkiness of the mass, which is visually evident in the surgical field.
The surgeon encounters significant adhesions in the posterior region, potentially related to a preoperative biopsy, while continuing the dissection around the mass.
The surgeon completes the robotic dissection and makes a fan and steel incision to finalize the mobilization, successfully removing the tumor intact.
The resected tumor is examined, revealing a bilobed mass with distinct solid and cystic components.
The patient recovered well after surgery and was discharged on postoperative day one.
Her final pathology demonstrated a completely excised, fifteen by ten by ten centimeter schwannoma.
Retrorectal or presacral tumors are an uncommon clinical entity.
They are typically found incidentally during routine physical exam, with the most common presenting symptom being a vague and chronic lower back or perineal pain.
They are more common in women and are typically cystic in nature, with the exception of chordomas, which are the most common solid tumor and have a slightly higher incidence in males.
Most retrorectal tumors are congenital and about half are benign, although some benign lesions do carry malignant potential.
In the large majority of cases, surgery, either via a transabdominal or perineal approach, is the mainstay of treatment.
The retrorectal space is a location where several different types of embryologically distinct cell lines converge, which leads to a wide variety of lesions in this area.
A treatment algorithm for the workup and management of these complex, yet rare tumors can be found in the ASCRS manual of colon and rectal surgery.
